-module(solve).
-export([solve/2]).

%% @doc
%% solve(Points, Target)
%% Finds the minimum generation k such that Target appears in generation 0 to k.
solve(Points, Target) ->
    case list_to_set(Points) of
        Set ->
            case is_member(Target, Set) of
                true -> 0;
                false ->
                    k = 0,
                    AllPoints = Set,
                    loop(k, AllPoints, Target)
            end
    end.

%% Helper function to check if an element is in a list (for convenience)
is_member(Element, List) ->
    lists:member(Element, List).

%% Recursive loop to simulate generations
loop(k, AllPoints, Target) when k >= 1 ->
    % Generate the next set of points (Generation k)
    NewPoints = generate_next_generation(AllPoints),

    % Check if Target is in the newly generated points
    case is_member(Target, NewPoints) of
        true -> k;
        false ->
            % Combine existing points with new ones (unique)
            NextAllPoints = list_to_set(AllPoints ++ NewPoints),
            % Recurse to the next generation
            loop(k + 1, NextAllPoints, Target)
    end.

%% Base case: Stop if no new points can be generated (e.g., only 1 unique point)
loop(_, AllPoints, _Target) ->
    -1.

%% Generates the next generation from the set of all previous points
generate_next_generation(AllPoints) ->
    % We need all unique pairs (a, b) from AllPoints where a != b
    Pairs = generate_unique_pairs(AllPoints),
    
    % Calculate the midpoint for each pair
    GeneratedPoints = [calculate_midpoint(A, B) || {A, B} <- Pairs],
    
    % Return the unique generated points
    list_to_set(GeneratedPoints).

%% Generates all unique pairs {A, B} from a list of points, where A and B are different points.
generate_unique_pairs(Points) ->
    N = length(Points),
    Pairs = [],
    % Iterate i from 1 to N
    Pairs = [
        {Points !! I, Points !! J}
        || I <- [0 .. N - 1],
           J <- [I + 1 .. N - 1]
    ].

%% Calculates the midpoint C = floor((A+B)/2)
calculate_midpoint(A, B) ->
    % A = [x1, y1, z1], B = [x2, y2, z2]
    X1 = A !! 0, Y1 = A !! 1, Z1 = A !! 2,
    X2 = B !! 0, Y2 = B !! 1, Z2 = B !! 2,
    
    % floor((a+b)/2) is equivalent to (a+b) div 2 for non-negative integers
    Cx = (X1 + X2) div 2,
    Cy = (Y1 + Y2) div 2,
    Cz = (Z1 + Z2) div 2,
    
    [Cx, Cy, Cz].

%% Utility function to convert a list of lists of integers (points) into a set representation for uniqueness tracking.
%% Since Erlang doesn't have a built-in hash set for lists of lists of ints, we use lists and rely on list_to_set behavior,
%% which groups identical terms. We must ensure that the elements being collected are canonical (i.e., sorted or consistently represented).
list_to_set(List) ->
    % Sort the list lexicographically to ensure that identical points hash/compare identically for set uniqueness.
    lists:sort(List).
